segments.text: Tena koutou katoa. Nga mihi ma ke tenei huihui, i tenei
ata.
Thank you very much everybody for your attendance today.
We are now starting the formal part of the day with regards
to our role as the Maori Affairs Select Committee
of Parliament itself. But first of all, thank you very much for the
mihi pakatone.
It's a very warm welcome to such a beautiful place that
you have here itself. And one of the treasures that we have as a
committee is to be able to get around the country and come to
places, to your whenua, and to enjoy your hospitality, but
also to see sometimes places that we've never been before.
And that's myself on this occasion.
This is the first time I've had the opportunity to be here, and I can tell you
I was excited to come, and I'm pleased that I have come.
So thank you for the very warm welcome to your whenua here
itself. So today, the process here is, is that
we as a select committee, we are here to hear from
those who submitted to the process of
this treaty settlement that we have in front of us here.
It is usual for us to hear those that are in support
of the treaty settlement, but it's not unusual for us to hear from people that
actually are not in support of the treaty settlement for whatever reason
they may have. So today is about us gaining a better
understanding of what the submissions are that are
coming towards us to take into account when we deliberate on
behalf of the settlement. Clearly, there's been
a long, you might argue, arduous timeframe
of actually getting it to this point here.
But nevertheless, this particular stage is an important
one for the Crown, because what we are talking about here is
creating law,
and law binds everybody towards it, including the Crown.
And I take words of some of the submissions here.
The settlement itself is the beginning of a
rebuild of an iwi of Ngā Pu,
of different places that we attend itself.
So we are very keen to hear from the submitters.
Today, we are looking at hearing from five parties
today. It's our convention that we always
start with the negotiating group to open up the
proceedings, but we also, as for fairness,
is to allow them to close up at some time at the end as well.
That's what we've always done as a committee, and we feel that that is a fair way.
We will also be having a hearing in Wellington also.
So there are some people that couldn't make their way to here today,
and for fairness, we want to make sure they have the opportunity also.
So we will be conducting a hearing in Wellington in a short time
as well. So the proceedings here, our convention,
everything is largely convention with regards to select committees, is that we
generally give groups 10 minutes to present and
individuals five minutes to present.
And we like to allow a bit of time for some questions as well
within those time frames. We've only got a short number today, so
as the Chair, I'll be a little bit flexible, but we do still have a
time to get by. And please excuse me if I have to
interrupt and move on to questions or to close the submission off at
that time. So aroha mai.
Now, we have a number of people that are present with us today that are
from Wellington and other places as well.
And of course, we heard from Matia, who spoke earlier on,
and Matia is here to help us with translation.
Not all of us are fluent in Te Reo. Some of us are, but not all of us are,
so it's very important that we understand, particularly if you wish
to use Te Reo in your presentation, and that's
absolutely accepted. Absolutely.
So we have a number of other staff from Te Tari Pakatone, from the Ministry of
Treaty Settlements, but also from our parliamentary services
staff who help us out as a committee to make sure.
There's now a bit of formality here.
It is a recorded meeting, so we record the meeting.
It's not Zoom, is it? It's actually just a recording today.
Obviously, usually we would actually have it publicly available.
This is a public meeting, so people are allowed to
come in here. Unless I feel that an individual or a grouping are
causing disruption to our process and not adding constructively to it,
then I have a role as Chair to manage that.
I've never had to do that, and I hope I don't have to do that
today. And it's also convention. You'll see that we've got a couple of
helpers here with Henare and also Jeff
for security. That's normal for us.
Parliamentary services who have a responsibility of looking after the
welfare of us all, but including the members of Parliament,
we just need to make sure that we've got ourselves being kept safe as well.
Our staff came here yesterday, and
apparently they moved these tables around a number of different ways to try and get
the best positioning to be able to conduct this.
So thank you to the staff for the work you've done, and I'm
sure it will work fine. So with that,
let me make sure there's nothing else I need to say.
I think I've covered most of it itself. Thank you once again.
We look very much forward to hearing what you have.
And first up, as I said before, we've got Te Pari
Taupoki, who have been doing the negotiations and all
that, and we'll open with your submission to begin with. So aroha.
Kia ora koutou.
So I'm submitting on behalf of the
Te Pari Taupoki, which isA
trust established
to receive the Deed of Settlement on behalf of Ngāti
Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana.
Ngāti Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana Settlement Trust submits
strong support of the Ngāti Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana Claims
Settlement Bill,
and urges the Justice Committee
to recommend that it's passed without amendment.
This settlement has been a long time coming,
going on 40 years.
Our people have waited generations for the Crown to acknowledge
what the Crown did to our people. The Deed of
Settlement represents a hard-won
negotiated
agreement made in good faith,
and the bill gives it legal effect.
We ask Parliament to honor that agreement.
The trust was established specifically to receive the settlement and hold its
benefits in trust
for current and future generations
of Ngāti Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana.
We are ready, our governance is in place,
and our pathway towards it
is prepared, and our people are waiting.
The historical account in the Deed records plainly and permanently
the serious Crown wrongs
committed against Ngāti Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana.
These include armed Crown attacks onto Kōpani and Onepoto in the
Its scorched earth tactics that destroyed our kaimei food
supplies.
Coercive and unlawful land dealings that stripped
Ngāti Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana
of our
Waikarimoana lands
under pressure and duress.
Broken promises of self-government under the Te Urewera
Reserve arrangements.
And decades of dispossession that left our people virtually
landless and severed from our ancestral rohe.
The Crown's
formal acknowledgments and apology in the Deed
recognizes these breaches of the Treaty of Waitangi and the
profound harms they caused.
Loss of life, loss of land, cultural dislocation, and
long-term socioeconomic damage across generations.
That is not simply a legal formality.
The public record and apology are vital for our people.
They validate the lived experience of our
kaumatua and confirm the truth of what happened.
They acknowledge the intergenerational harm,
trauma
that continues to affect our whānau today.
They provide a foundation for
restoring honor and building a just,
ongoing relationship between the Crown and Ngāti Rūpani Mai
Waikarimoana.
Most importantly, this history,
told and recorded by us, becomes a permanent record.
Our mokopuna will always know who they are, where they come from,
and why this redress matters.
The cultural redress in this settlement is significant and in
important respects unique.
The transfer of lands into Te Urewera reflects a clear and
principled shift away from the Crown's model of private
ownership
to a framework that recognizes Te Urewera as a legal entity in its
own right,
and affirms Ngāti Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana's role as kaitiaki,
which has always been a truth.
Our mana and kaitiakitanga over this rohe were
never extinguished.
They were suppressed by Crown actions.
The Te Urewera Act model corrects that.
Whakapapa becomes the legal foundation for access, use, and
responsibility,
entirely consistent with how our tūpuna
understood and exercised authority over this rohe since
time immemorial.
The settlement also transfers specific sites, including
Turiōkahu and Kaitawa,
as Crown properties into Te Urewera.
These sites provide places for housing and reconnection to our whānau.
The governance arrangements
embed our decision-making and tikanga alongside
statutory protections,
enabling conservation, visitor use, and community
needs to be managed together
in a way that reflects our values.
In short, the settlement gives legal expression
to what has always been
true, that Ngāti Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana, as a hapū of Tūhoe,
holds responsibilities of kaitiakitanga within Te Urewera.
It
creates a durable framework for that authority to be exercised,
recognized, and honored into the future.
Ngāti Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana is a hapū of Tūhoe.
That whakapapa relationship is not administrative, it is who we
are.
It defines our identity, our obligations to this rohe,
and the tikanga under which we exercise our kaitiakitanga within
Te Urewera.
Our relationship with Tūhoe is one of whanaungatangaAnd it's
from that foundation that we act as tangata whenua of Waipara
Moana.
To who have supported this settlement,
that support is not incidental. It reflects a shared whakapapa
understanding of the responsibilities and authority that belong to the people of
this rohe.
It is that living relationship, not administrative
structures, that will continue to guide
how we care for Waipara Moana and Te Irurewa.
As required by the Treaty settlement process, we engaged with all
neighboring iwi throughout the negotiation of this settlement.
Those conversations were conducted in
good faith
and are a matter of record.
Where the iwi have raised concerns about this settlement, we acknowledge those
views
while respectfully disagreeing.
Our authority over this rohe is grounded in whakapapa and in the
continuity of our presence and responsibilities as ahika.
That is not a position we arrive at through negotiation.
It is not one we will depart from.
Other iwi have had the opportunity through the Treaty settlement process to achieve
redress to their claims.
We do not accept that our settlement is the appropriate vehicle
for relitigating their outcomes.
Financial and commercial redress.
The trust acknowledges honestly that
no financial settlement can fully compensate for the scale of
what was taken from Ngati Ruapani Mai Waipara Moana.
The losses of land
and of economic capacity across generations cannot be
reduced to a dollar figure.
What the financial and commercial redress does provide is a
foundation.
It gives us the tools to begin rebuilding
the economic strength and prosperity our people had
before being disenfranchised from our lands
and customary rights.
The trust is prepared to deploy these assets purposefully,
to grow them into jobs, services, and intergenerational wealth for
our people.
Partners have been identified, plans are in place,
and we will report transparently on outcomes to our iwi.
The settlement does not end our journey.
It begins
the rebuilding.
We wish to speak plainly to the committee about what continued delay means
for our people.
We have carried this hurt for too long.
The weight of unresolved historical grievance has worn down our values,
our beliefs, and our relationships with one another.
Each year of inaction
brings more disconnection from our rohe,
more lost opportunities to invest in our whanau and rangatahi,
a deeper harm to the wairua of our community.
Our kaumatua have waited their whole lives,
and many have passed away now.
Whole lives for this acknowledgment.
Our rangatahi deserve to inherit a settlement, empowered identity,
and not a legacy of unresolved grievance.
The bill is the first essential step toward that restoration.
Passing it now means we can begin the real work.
Healing relationships, restoring kaitiakitanga over Waipara
Moana, strengthening our whanau well-being, and building a
stable future.
Further delay will deepen the harm.
There is no benefit for the Crown or to our people in
prolonging the process.
The Ngati Ruapani Mai Waipara Moana Settlement Trust respectfully requests
that the Justice Committee
recommend that the Ngati Ruapani Mai Waipara Moana Claims Settlement Bill
be passed without amend,
acknowledging its report, the significance of the historical record,
and Crown apology to the people of Ngati Ruapani Mai Waipara Moana.
Prioritize the bill's passage so that the work of restoration and
rebuilding can begin without further delay.
I will
take a couple of short questions if there are any now, but understanding we'll have
another opportunity at the end of our hearing to ask further questions.
But are there any immediate questions that we have from members at this time?
Kei
te pai.
Yeah?
Ripeka.
Ripeka.
These questions from the floor.
Are you asking a question?
Yes.
Unfortunately, well, the process is here for us to ask
questions. We don't turn into cross-examination.
I meant is Ripeka part of one of the other submissions?
She's part of our submission.
Yeah, so you'll get an opportunity to talk to what you wish to then, okay?
So but with regards, thank you very much, Kara, for that
verbal submission itself, and we'll be seeing you again later on. So thank you.
Kia ora.
Kia ora.
Kia ora.
So we'll move now to Ngati Ruapani Ki Uta ki Tai
Co-op and Dr. Rapata Wiri.
Tena koe.
Kia ora koutou.
So Rapata,
we've
got
10 minutes allocated there.
So you use that how you see fit. We have had the opportunity to read the written
submission, but you use the time as you wish to. Kia ora.
My name is Dr. Rapata Wiri, and I speak on behalf of
Ngati Ruapani Ki Uta ki Tai Cooperative
Society.
I am one of seven directors.
We were set up under the Companies Act to unify all
Ngati Ruapani from Waikaremoana to Wairoa
to Gisborne, because our tipuna Ruapani is actually
from Gisborne.
The current submitters, Te Whare Taupoki,
represent Ngati Ruapani mai Waikaremoana,
affectionately referred to by Alistair Best in
his book, "Tuhou:
Children of the Mist."
We represent Ngati Ruapani ki
Waikaremoana
who are effectively
the children who got missed out.
So the difference between mai Waikaremoana
and ki Waikaremoana
is fundamental
to this proposed bill.
First, we vehemently
oppose the proposed bill passed on the fact that its
foundations,
its framework, are seriously flawed.
In particular, I draw your attention to Te Whare Taupoki
submission which states that Ngati Ruapani is a
hapu of Tuhou.
We have proven beyond all reasonable doubt in the Waitangi
Tribunal hearing of 2004 that Ngati
Ruapani are not a hapu of Tuhou.
Ruapani,
this lodge here, was named after Ruapani
as a male ancestor from Gisborne who came here.
He's six generations from the waka Horouta
which landed in Gisborne.
Kia ora.
Tuhou comes from another waka called
Matatua.
Tuhou
comes from Whakatane.
Kia ora.
So there is no whakapapa connection-
Kia ora
... between Ruapani and Tuhou.
Ae.
So from the outset, the
submission by Te Whare Taupoki is flawed in terms of its
own whakapapa.
To clarify, there are two entities at the core of this debate.
This settlement, proposed settlement only caters for those
Ruapani who whakapapa to Tuhou,
and does not cater for those Ruapani who
whakapapa to Kahungunu to all the land in between here and
Wairoa to Gisborne,
and who have land rights in the six blocks that are being transferred to
Te Urewera Board,
which is a Tuhou board.
That's what's proposed under this bill.
Therefore, this bill must be urgently
halted.
This bill is inconsistent with the Crown's treaty obligations of active
protection
and good faith.
The whakapapa and history of the submitters,
Te Whare Taupoki, who I've just discovered are the PSGE, we
had no idea they're going to receive this $24 million
plus the six blocks of land,
is incorrect
and it is Tuhou centric.
By passing this bill, you are validating false whakapapa
and a fabricated history
of Ngati Ruapani mai Waikaremoana, which is essentially a
Tuhou biased history.
Kia ora.
Even the Maori translation
of this report is Tuhou biased. It's written in
the Tuhou dialect.
We oppose the bill
vehemently as we argue that false whakapapa
and false historical accounts cannot be validated by the
Crown
or its agencies or by its
ministers.
In their whakapapa and historical accounts which are documented in this
settlement,
the history of Ruapani,
Ngati Ruapani, is confused and influenced by an
ideology called Te Mana Motuhake o
Tuhou
which states, believes that Ngati Ruapani is a hapu of Tuhou,
fought alongside Tuhou to define this beautiful Te Urewera,
Te Rohe Pōtae o Tuhou,
the Urewera District Native Reserve in 1896.
However, if you read their historical account, the history that
they have written
fails to acknowledge that Ngati Ruapani withdrew from Te
Rohe Pōtae o Tuhou in 1899.
We withdrew,
and we formed our own Mana Motuhake
as Ruapani in 1899 and again
in 1917 with the Urewera Consolidation Scheme
when we formed the reserves. The 12 reserves up at Lake Waikaremoana
are only for Ngati Ruapani ki Waikaremoana.
Tuturu Ngati Ruapani. 317
Ngati Ruapani
owners.
We have a list of owners.
I have argued before the Maori Land Court last year when
the submitters, the
guy who did your pohiri this morning, said that the meeting house
up the land, the marae up here belongs to Tuhou.
Well, I objected to that
based on the fact that Te Mana Motuhake o Tuhou is
nothing more than ideological mumbo
jumboTherefore, the whakapapa and
historical account presented by Te Whare Taupoki must not be
validated by the select committee.
I ask the select committee to pause.
Kaiwhakahawe. We oppose the transfer of the six
blocks
to Te Urewera Board
and argue that this bill contradicts Tikanga Māori.
And I refer to case law.
Case law, the Supreme Court, the case of Pakinekora
and Te Kaunihera Kaumatua o Tūhoe versus Te Wī
Taumataua in 2023,
where the Supreme Court ruled that Tikanga cannot be its
own judge.
This is a Supreme Court decision,
therefore breach of Tikanga cannot be
validated by this select committee.
This bill is also a breach of tino rangatiratanga guaranteed in Article
2nd of the Treaty,
and
mana motuhake,
which Mr Trader Tate and I, my uncle,
argued before the Constitutional Kaupapa Inquiry in Palmerston North
last year.
You can read our evidence at pages 31 and 32 of the
draft report of the Constitutional Kaupapa Inquiry,
where we rejected
the constitutional legitimacy of unilateral Crown
sovereignty,
which allows laws such as this proposed
bill to be passed without consultation.
This proposed bill creates enduring
legal prejudice.
We also oppose the bill because the Crown has relied on one
group's mandate,
Ngati Ruapani mai Waikaremoana, the Children of the Mist.
The Crown has failed to properly resolve
overlapping claims with our whanau, with our hapu,
and the six blocks that have been transferred to
Te Urewera Board.
This is inconsistent with tribunal guidance that overlapping
interests must be properly addressed,
not overridden or ignored as some sort of intertribal
dispute.
In that submission, Te Whare Taupoki
claims that the history is plain and permanent.
However, we argue that the history is not as plain and permanent and
simple as they might lead you to believe.
The historical account they have provided is very much an
unsettled history.
The historical account is Tūhoe-centric.
Tūhoe-centric.
Tūhoe bias
treats Ngati Ruapani as a hapu. We are not a hapu of
Tūhoe. We are an iwi in our own right.
The historical account has not been agreed upon by all
affected parties. You heard I read the submissions from
our cousins from Ngati Kahungunu,
Tatau-Tatau.
Karahora. Karahora. We have one here who's going to give evidence
today.
At risk of becoming legislated history,
this creates a dangerous precedent because disputed
historical narratives
become permanently embedded in law,
and I believe in the rule of law.
I gained a Master of Laws from Victoria University.
I don't believe in the law of the jungle. I believe in the rule of law.
We oppose the bill because Te Urewera Board and land
authority issues.
There are issues with the board. The bill
embeds redress within Te Urewera framework,
effectively reinforcing Tūhoe governance structures,
limits recognition of other iwi authorities and
PSTEs such as Tatau-Tatau.
It locks in a fabricated interpretation of mana whenua,
and that is based on
lies
and the manipulation of whakapapa.
Dr. Witty, could you just point out any new outcomes?
We've sort of covered a little bit that you-
Yeah
... a bit repetitive, but if you could just point out-
Okay
... any new stuff you've got there.
So, just very quickly.
So we've been denied equal recognition.
We're structurally excluded. The prejudice is severe and
irreversible,
and the bill will cause permanent loss of mana whenua,
loss of treaty claim rights, distortion of whakapapa, and entrenchment of
incorrect historical narratives.
If I may just talk briefly about delay.
Very briefly, yes.
Very briefly. Te Whare Taupoki argues that delay will cause us harm.
We oppose this because we believe that a rushed settlement will create a flawed
settlement, and a flawed settlement will cause far greater
harm than any delay. Recommendations,
and I close up here.
Yeah.
We respectfully request that this bill will not and cannot proceed in its
current form.
We request that an independent review of whakapapa
assertions, historical account, mandate, and agreement in principle.
We request genuine engagement with Ngati Ruapani
ki Waikaremoana claimants and community,
as well as Tatau-Tatau and other affected
PSTEsWe respectfully urge the
select committee to delay
and pause the progress of the bill
until these concerns have been independently examined
and resolved.
A treaty settlement founded upon unresolved
grievances cannot deliver true and enduring
justice.
We are not opposed to treaty settlements,
but we urge the select committee
to pause
on passing this bill
in order to make the foundations of this treaty settlement
more solid,
more robust,
more transparent,
and fairer for everyone concerned.
Kia ora.
Ngā mihi.
Thank you very much.
Just before you move off this, are there any questions of Dr.
Ruby?
Yes. Steve?
Hey, got a question.
Steve.
Yeah.
Kia ora.
Kia
ora.
I appreciate that.
You would know from the legal process around these settlements that
the effect of the
Māori Affairs Select Committee's responsibility
is to formalize
a deed of settlement that's been already signed.
And
traditionally, it's
peculiar or unusual to delay this, so
you're making the case for an exceptional circumstance.
Obviously, there's often disagreement within iwi about whether
a settlement should be made, whether it's sufficient, and we understand,
we are acutely aware at least that, I feel acutely, the divisive character
of settlements often. It's why I've just stood market want to, because
I've circulated that divisive settlement processes.
Mm-hmm.
I wonder if
understanding your
profoundly expressed concerns,
do you think there's a version of meaningful
amendments that could be made that would resolve them, or is it really
the fundamental character of it which places Maere
Ruapani as a part of Tuhoe
when you believe that Ruapani should be as
independent to Tuhoe?
The settlement should be separate from any association with the Tuhoe reo.
So I feel like that was really a clarification for understanding.
I think you made it pretty clear.
Yeah. Thank you.
Kia ora.
Okay. That's order.
Kush? Kushal?
Tena koe, otirā, tēnā tatou katoa kua huihui mai i tēnei rā.
You mentioned the requirement for clarification of whakapapa.
Yes.
And I think we can all agree that that is not the role of the Crown or this select
committee. How do you envisage that happening, that process, and who might that
group be?
I think there needs to be meaningful engagement between Ngati
Ruapani mai i Waikaremoana or te whare kaupiki
and Ngati Ruapani ki Waikaremoana and tatou
katoa
to address those overlapping issues of whakapapa.
They haven't been meaningfully
discussed.
And Ira will
talk more about that. That korero hasn't occurred.
So you
need a period of time, you're requesting a period of time for an independent
process driven by respective hapu iwi.
Aye.
What might that timeframe be?
Six months.
Kia ora.
Tena koe. Any last questions of Dr.?
Kia ora, Doctor. Thank you very much for your impassioned submission.
We appreciate it.
Thank you.
And I think you've given us a lot of thought
to be considered in our deliberations we're heading into.
Kia ora.
Kia ora mai.
Kia ora.
Kia ora koutou.
Okay. Can we now move to Tāhuri o te Rangi,
Traynor Tate?
Tena koe.
Kia ora koutou.
Traynor, we actually have you down for-
Sorry, I'm a bit-
Sorry. We actually have you down for five minutes as an individual, but
like I said before, I'll be a little bit flexible.
But, we've had the opportunity to read your written submission.
So you point out the key points you want us to understand.
So kia ora mai.
Kia ora. I don't think it's going to last more than five minutes, but however,
opposition to the bill.
I'm sorry, I've written everything in te reo.
Hey, ae.
Hei, hei te pai.
Nā tēnei pire e tono ana kia uru mai te
papahātepai ki te whakahaere mō Te Urewera.
He tino māharahara tēnei.
He hoki whakamuri tēnei. He whakahokinga mai i te
mana ki te karauna.
He whakaiti i te mana o te tangata whenua.
Kāore tēnei i te hāngai ki te wairua o Te Urewera hei
tangata. Kāore hoki i te hāngai ki te whakaaro o te
rangatiratanga. Rangatiratanga and whakapapa.
He uri
nō tēnei whenua. He tino māharahara ana ahau
i te whakaiti i te mana o te tangata whenua.
Ko ngā whakatau mō Te Urewera, me noho ki te
hunga i whai whakapapa ana ki a ia.
Kāore he tikaKia nekeia atu
taua mana ki te Crown.
Via representation. Nei tētahi take nui.
Kāore he māngai mō te rohe o Wairoa ki runga i te
poari o Te Urewera.
Kāore he reo. Ahakoa ngā hononga whakapapa,
ahakoa ngā kōrero tuku iho, kāore
mātou i te tēpi.
Kāore e tika kia whakarere kēhia te
hānganga
mēnā kāore anō kia whakatikahia
tēnei raru.
Te Tiriti o Waitangi. E pā ana hoki tēnei ki Te
Tiriti o Waitangi. Kei hea te rangatiratanga,
te whakawhanaungatanga,
te tiakitanga o te mana whenua?
Ki ahau nei kāore e kitea ana i tēnei
pire.
I roto i ō mātou hapori,
kei reira tonu ngā mamae o mua.
Ngā wā i tangohia ai te mana, ngā wā
kāore mātou i rango.
Ae, kei te kite anō mātou i aua āhuatanga i roto
i tēnei pire.
Ko tāku nei i hiahia.
Nō reira, e te komiti, kaua e whakaae
ki tēnei pire i tēnei āhua, engari
whakaritea he māngai mō te rohe o
Wairoa. Waiho te mana whakahaere ki te tangata
whenua.
Whai i ngā mātāpono o Te Tiriti o
Waitangi.
Hei whakakaci i tēnei kōrero,
ko Te Urewera ehara i te rawa, he tangata, he tipuna.
Ko
mātou ngā uri. Kei a mātou te kawenata ki te
tiaki i a ia.
Kāore au e whakaae kia
whakaitihia tōna mana.

iwi and apu with what they've gone through
in that. So it is quite an emotional journey that I say personally for
us to actually go through this here.
But in closing, thank you
for the openness of everybody that submitted today on both
sides of the argument itself. Thank you
for being respectful to one another.
I actually was remiss in saying at the beginning, I always try to say it's
important you use the rugby term, play the ball, not the
man.
This is about the kaupapa. It's not about the personalities, and
as far as I could see today, that was respectfully done by all the
submitters, so I appreciate that-
Yeah
... in itself.
Also, it's obviously appropriate for me to thank all the
people that have made this so welcoming for us to come and
conduct our official responsibilities here
on your whenua here. Thank you, Craig, for making such a long journey to
participate, too. I acknowledge that. Thank you, too. Yeah.
And thank you to the staff. The staff play an important part of making our
job easier,
and also the people that keep us safe in this environment that you've
got here. Thank you for my first opportunity to be here.
You have a beautiful part of the world.
It was a lovely journey that we had this morning from Wairoa and through
your wonderful valleys to here. It's a beautiful place, and I hope to bring my
wider whanau here back here at some stage in the future as well because it's
a special place with that. But with that, I bring this official part of
our proceedings today to a close, and may I ask
you perhaps to say maybe a closing karakia for us today.
Kia ora.
Ka oti ake i te kakaha, ka hokia mai te mauri o te korero ki a koe, ki te
hau kāinga, ki te
āhika. Ka whakatau i a koutou, ko koutou whakatangatu.
Kei a Waireti pea ngā whakamārama mō ngā whakahaere ā muri ake
nei. Me tatari kia mā ngā koutou
tohutohu. Heoi ano,
rawe ngā whakahaere a koutou, ngā
minitā, ngā kaihautū o te Kāwanan.
He miomatuakore, heke i hoko e pau ki te whenua, ko Papatūānuku te
aitana ma. Mauitikitiki a Taranga.
Ka p**a ko te uwha, ka p**a ko ngā kaupapa o tōna ao.
He mauri, he tapu, he ihi ana. Ka p**a ko te iratara ta ki te pae ao, ki
te ao mārama. Tihei Mauri Ora!
Kia ora mai, and kia ora tatou katoa
